data.title: DICK'S Sporting Goods Hits Home Run In Retail With Buyout Of Foot Locker
data.description:
Although I often cover retail REITs and invest in some, today I'm taking a slightly different angle by writing about an actual retail brand, and that is DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS), whose brand focuses on sporting goods equipment, fitness equipment, golf equipment, fishing gear products, and apparel.

Considering their next earnings result comes out on March 12th, about a month away, this is an opportune time to review this stock holistically.

Interestingly, it seems to have beat several recent quarterly earnings estimates, and it only dropped around 2% or so during Thursday's huge market selloff, while some other stocks sunk over 15%. So, today's article also takes a closer look at the potential for retail in 2026.

Thesis Summary: Bullish

My article today calls this stock a buy, driven by several factors including macro resilience and strong competitive positioning in its space, as well as growth within its brand concepts and a major acquisition, but also future upside potential in relation to valuation. This is despite technical indicators that are somewhat less bullish.

My worksheet below summarizes what factors contributed to the score:

Keep reading to learn more about each section and what drove my view.

Macro & Sector Outlook: Bullish

To start off with, let's consider the US retail sector and the role consumer spending confidence plays.

Some positive but muted outlooks have emerged on retail. For instance, in January, Bain & Co. wrote that it "expects US retail sales to grow 3.5% year over year in 2026, a slight slowdown from 2025's expected 4.0%," adding that "ongoing geopolitical and economic uncertainty is weighing on consumer confidence."

Additionally, Deloitte conducted a survey of retail executives for 2026 and the majority seemed to expect YoY revenue growth and increased operating margins this year:

Both of these outlooks could add to investor and analyst confidence in retail overall, driving upside to retailers' share prices potentially.

The other key development in this sector is the role of AI and retailers' increased investment in it. Industry association Natl Retail Federation wrote in January:

The lion's share of capital will go toward customer relationship management and personalization tools that can analyze shopper data to tailor offerings, chatbots and virtual assistants, predictive analytics for demand forecasting, and supply chain optimization.

As someone who has been following artificial intelligence/AI trends for a few years now, both via in-person seminars/talks as well as online research on the topic, my perspective is that we should keep in mind that "retail" also includes a vast e-commerce portfolio, and not just brick-and-mortar stores, so where I really see the value is in retailers' e-commerce presence and ability to funnel a customer to just the product they want.

This saves the customer time, increases the chance of buying just what they want, and improves brand loyalty since it could streamline the process. On the backend, there is the potential for more cost efficiencies (customer service/contact centers, supply chain/logistics, etc.) so let's not just throw away the AI argument just yet.

With those points made above, I am a buy in this category.

Competitive Positioning: Highly Bullish

Next, I want to talk about the competitive positioning of DICK'S within the retail sector.

A major development in this sector is DICK'S completing the acquisition of Foot Locker last year. Just consider what it does to DICK'S brand positioning and reach. According to the press release,

As a combined company, DICK'S will now operate more than 3,200 stores plus e-commerce and digital businesses across 20 countries in North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, plus a licensed store presence in Europe, the Middle East and Asia.

That already moves the needle towards the upside. However, it is also worth considering brand competition in the retail niche of sporting goods, so I picked 3 peers who have a considerable presence in that space: Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO), Tractor Supply Co. (TSCO), and Sportsman's Warehouse (SPWH). The market cap range of this peer group is $48MM to $35B, so a broad spectrum.

Notable from peer data is that DKS had a 5-year revenue CAGR of +10.4%, the best in the group, and also achieved the 2nd best return on equity/ROE in this peer group, at 23.7%.

Those are both impressive figures that can only add to the confidence in this stock. I would also point out that momentum data on Seeking Alpha shows DKS beating a major benchmark like the S&P500 in price return over the last 10-year period, and also beating this entire peer group selected.

Thus, the evidence above overwhelmingly justifies a strong buy in this section.

Company Growth Factors: Highly Bullish

When it comes to company growth factors, if you follow my articles on retail REITs, you'll know I look for portfolio growth, which means new stores acquired or developed. That can be the case for the actual retail brands too, like DICK'S.

We already mentioned the acquisition of Foot Locker, which is already a major business growth lever.

Further, the company is growing its House of Sports concept, saying in its Q3 investor deck (pg. 12) that "we plan to have as many as 75 to 100 House of Sport locations by the end of FY27."

As someone who just loves "immersive" brand experiences, whether they be a hotel/casino, a restaurant concept, or a retail chain, one potential growth driver that got my attention is the company's Golf Galaxy concept, which has been growing, and I think this can drive future revenues. According to the Q3 investor deck (pg 15), "We currently have 112 Golf Galaxy locations, including Performance Centers, 8 of which we opened in FY25."

Adding to the bullish case here is Seeking Alpha peer data showing DKS at FWD revenue growth of +18.8%, the highest in the peer group.

Of all the points mentioned, however, I think the greatest weight to the growth story should come from the Foot Locker acquisition, which is significant, and with that said, I gave this category a strong buy.

Profitability Metrics: Neutral

The metric of choice today, considering this is retail, is the operating margin, and here is a comparison vs. peers:

Here is yet another metric where DKS dominates, with an operating margin of just over +10%, beating all its peers.

This indicates to me a strong earnings efficiency in the core business, in a sector not known for terribly high margins, and considering the overhead of running so many physical stores.

What I'll be looking for in the upcoming earnings call in March is that the prior upgraded EPS guidance has been met or exceeded. From the Q3 press release (pg. 1), the company said it "raises full year 2025 earnings per diluted share guidance for the DICK'S business to a range of $14.25 to $14.55, up from $13.90 to $14.50 previously."

Of course, a merger with Foot Locker is underway, so in the near term I do expect integration costs and at least some integration challenges, which can be expected. In fact, in the Q3 statement, company said integration/merger-related costs "are expected to result in future pre-tax charges of $500 to 750MM."

I think that ought to smooth out in the long term, however.

In terms of net margin, DKS is among the top two in this peer group, and I think if that trend continues, it could positively drive future earnings.

However, to consider another angle, consensus estimates point to an EPS decline for the fiscal year ending Jan 2026, and a return to EPS growth in FY27 and FY28. This may hurt a strong bullish consensus in the near term, along with merger/integration costs, so I am neutral in this category despite the impressive margins.

Balance Sheet Risk: Bullish

Next, let's consider some balance sheet risk topics.

First, here is a comparison of the debt-to-equity vs. peers, showing that DKS is in the lower/middle range of this peer group, so not a major outlier or one that has significantly higher leverage than the peer group, and that is another positive:

More good news is that this company is also getting rating agency coverage, with S&P Global giving its senior unsecured debt issuance an investment-grade BBB rating last September.

To add another angle to this story, I turned to the "current ratio" which shows if near-term assets can cover near-term obligations, and DKS is mostly in line with its peer group in that regard, with all of them being above 1.0.

Given these multiple points above, I am bullish in this section as well.

Dividend Outlook: Bullish

Moving on, let's talk about dividends.

Unlike some retail REITs, this is a retail chain, so it is not really a high-yield stock, with a yield under 3%, in line with peers. Compare that with FrontView REIT, a retail REIT, whose yield is above 5%.

However, yield alone is not everything. DKS has a payout ratio around 33%, so it is attractive in that regard, and this leaves lots of cash wiggle room in the event of a downturn.

Where it really wins for me is the proven long-term dividend growth story, achieving a 10-year dividend CAGR of +24%, beating its sector average.

This ought to get the interest of dividend investors, so I am bullish in this category.

Valuation Metrics: Highly Bullish

In this section, I made the following worksheet to present valuation metrics and a price forecast, using metrics from Seeking Alpha data:

What this worksheet can tell us is that DKS is very slightly undervalued compared to the peer group average when it comes to FWD P/E (GAAP) and more undervalued in terms of the price/book value. Considering its strong profit margins mentioned earlier, I would call this an undervaluation opportunity, which also shows 27% potential further upside in my price forecast.

It is a strong buy in this category for me.

Technical Charting: Neutral

Lastly, let's also consider some technical chart patterns:

If using very basic technical concepts like buy signals and sell signals, we see where the 50-day SMA crossed above the longer-term 200-day SMA last summer, forming a buy signal. Since then, the two moving averages have been squeezing. So, from a purely technical view, I consider this a hold/wait pattern and no clear buy or sell signal right now.

This neutral pattern lowers the overall score for the stock somewhat, but the bullish factors fundamentally are still overwhelming.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

The biggest challenge to my bullish thesis, considering this sector is a consumer discretionary sector, would be a major and extended recession that includes a huge drop in consumer spending confidence. That is the one thing I think could have the greatest negative weight on this type of business and its entire sector because it literally depends on consumer confidence.

Some major financial firms following recession risk have not been overly concerned, though, with Fidelity writing in January that "despite signs of weakness in some areas, the state of the US economy in 2026 remains strong."

A mitigating factor in this case could be the size and scope of DICK'S, in the wake of buying out Foot Locker, and we have seen this company emerge from major market shocks like the global pandemic of 2020/2021, indicating its resilience.

Conclusion

In summary, my article today agrees with the latest Wall St. consensus and calls this stock a buy.

I've shown why this firm is a growth idea as well as a dividend idea, and why the Foot Locker acquisition is very material to this business and its growth thesis.

To use a sporting analogy, when it comes to this stock, I see it as a home run!

data.title: Yum China Holdings: Quarter And FYE Results Were Tasty Indeed! Reiterating A Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction

In my first foundation piece, I detailed the basics of my thesis which included: Yum China Holdings' (YUMC) wide economic moat as the dominant retailer in China's fast food market, led by the very popular KFC brand; an experienced and savvy management team that has constructed a very efficient supply chain with a high degree of leverage over suppliers; an aggressive business model that has shifted growth to medium and smaller Chinese cities where it has little to no exposure; the increasing use of a higher margin franchise model for new store openings; and finally a loyalty program with nearly 600 thousand registered customers.

I mention two additional important points for prospective investors or those already owning the shares. First is that this is a relatively young company, spun off from US based YUM Brands in 2016. Although its roots date back to YUM's opening of the first Chinese KFC in 1987, it was only after the spin-off that YUMC gained the identity it has today. This contrasts with McDonald's, which dates to 1955; Wendy's to 1969; Burger King to 1954; and Popeyes to 1972.

Its current CEO, Joey Wat (52), joined YUMC shortly before the spin-off and became CEO in 2018. Educated in Hong Kong and at the Northwestern School of Business in Chicago, and trained in consultancy at McKinsey and Company, Wat has instilled a Chinese-style regional flair in the "Western-style" fast food within the current YUMC menus. She has also accelerated the pace of a business model shift from company owned stores to a franchise model, which should enhance the efficiency of the firm in the coming years.

The second point is the efficiency of the company's supply chain. Remember its first unit opened in 1987 during a time when Chinese infrastructure was primitive and subcontractors were virtually nonexistent. To facilitate its growth, the company was forced to invest in the development of its own proprietary logistic system, from procurement of ingredients (close relationships and control over poultry suppliers), to room temperature, refrigerated, and frozen storage to delivery with a fleet of over 2000 distribution vehicles. Its far-flung array of 35 logistic distribution systems (even able to service stores in the most remote corners of Tibet) has been gradually updated to employ full automation with heavy use of robotics.

TABLE 1

TABLE 2

The loyalty system of nearly 600 million members, although not global, dwarfs other programs.

TABLE 3

Recent Relative Price Performance

YUMC has recently begun to surge in price - the beneficiary of the market's favorable view of "hard asset" companies whose products cannot be disrupted/displaced by AI. These so-called H.A.L.O. companies ("hard assets, low obsolescence") are very much in vogue in today's market. From its 52-week low of $43 on April 17, it is up nearly 31% to a close of $54 on 2/13/26. So far in 2026, it is up 15%.

Although possibly somewhat overbought currently, the firm has a consistency of sales and earnings growth seen over the last few years that makes it quite attractive for 15-20% total returns in the near future.

TABLE 4

Fourth Quarter and FYE 12/2025 Details

Summary

YUMC reported solid results for Q4, which capped off a very good year. Non-GAAP EPS was $0.40/sh, beating the street estimates by $0.03. Total system sales were up 7% Y/Y with same-store sales growing 3% Q/Q and 1% Y/Y for KFC and 1% Q/Q and Y/Y for Pizza Hut. The company opened 1700 net new stores, bringing the total store count to over 18,000. The new store openings were a record for the firm for one year. YUMC also demonstrated in the quarter that it can continue to innovate, launching over 600 new or upgraded items in 2025.

Expansion into lower-tier cities continued with rapid progress, led by the side-by-side KFC/Pizza Hut concept named "Gemini". This inexpensive, low-CAPEX model is ideal for entry into 3rd-tier cities and attractive for franchisees due to its lower upfront costs. Only 40 have been opened thus far, but the company expects the rollout to accelerate quickly in the coming years.

CEO Wat commented in the conference call on YUMC's growth prospects in China:

Recently, I visited Chongqing, China's largest city... with over 30 million people....I saw a strong appetite for affordable, good food. KFC's density there is only 4 stores per million people, well below the average of 17 in tier 1 and 2 cities, or Shanghai's 28.

I am convinced that this well-managed firm with its very popular, dominant brands can continue or even accelerate its recent growth pattern into the future.

Management rewarded shareholders with a whopping +21% increase in its quarterly dividend to $0.29/sh, giving the shares a 2.03% yield at the closing price of $57 on 2/10/2026. Shareholders can expect a continued accelerated pace of dividend growth. YUMC has a pristine balance sheet with no net debt, and its efficient operations throw off consistently growing free cash flow. Management's move to a higher percentage of franchised stores in new openings will allow the firm to conserve capital spending and thus enhance FCF even further.

The hefty dividend increase was part of a larger plan to accelerate capital returns to shareholders, with the firm expecting to return a total of $1.5 billion in capital in 2026 alone, representing about 9% of its total market capitalization. CEO Wat stated:

[We are] committed to maintaining a dual focus: driving business growth and delivering favorable returns to shareholders. With a healthy cash position and cash-generation capabilities, we are on track to return US$4.5 billion to shareholders from 2024 through 2026. Starting in 2027...we plan to return approximately 100% of annual free cash flow after subsidiaries' dividend payments to non-controlling interests.

This prospective 9% return via dividend and stock purchases in 2026 is even more impressive when compared to the recent annual returns from members of the S&P 500. A mid 2025 study by SA author David Templeton, "S&P Index Buybacks Hit Record", indicated that the 12-month dividend and buyback yield for the S&P components totaled 3.27% through March of 2025. YUMC's prospective yield of 9% for 2026 is truly remarkable, a testament to its economic moat, efficiency, and dedication to its stakeholders.

Income Statement

Already mentioned was the impressive topline growth in the quarter and year. In my opinion, an oft overlooked, but important performance indicator was within the quarterly and annual expense items. For company restaurants, operating expense items (as a % of restaurant sales) were impressive: food and paper down 7% and 2% for the quarter and year; payroll and employee benefits down 13% and 7%; in total, company unit expenses as a % of company restaurant sales were down 7% for the quarter and 3% for the year. This led to company restaurant margins of 13% for the quarter (up 70 bp over 2024), and 16.3% for the year (up 60 bp). Overall operating margins were up 80 bp to 6.6% (quarter) and up 60 bp to 10.9% (annual). GAAP EPS increased 7% Y/Y, and a hefty 33% Q/Q handily beating the street estimates.

TABLE 5

Statement of Cash Flows

Although the increase in cash from operating activity was a modest 3.3% in 2025, capital spending dropped by 11.2%, thus freeing up more cash for discretionary use. Free cash flow (FCF) was up an impressive 17.6% in 2025, and YUMC management was generous in returning it to shareholders in the form of dividends and share repurchases. A total of $1.6B was distributed to stakeholders in both 2025 and 2024, with total dividends paid on common stock up a whopping 42% in 2025! Remember, this is indicative of a wide economic moat, a dominant market position, efficient operations, highly sought-after products; all of these characteristics add up to an FCF production machine!

TABLE 6

Balance Sheet

Not much to say here. The balance sheet remains virtually debt-free with net debt of only $520 million and $148 million as of FYE 2025 and 2024, respectively, against total equity of $6.1 billion and $6.4 billion. Total cash plus short-term investments represents almost 13% of total assets as of 12/2025.

TABLE 7

KFC and Pizza Hut - Summary Results

KFC

A very solid quarter for KFC with system sales growth (+8% for the quarter and 5% for the year); same store sales growth (+3% quarter, 1% annual), operating margins (+60bp to 10.5% quarter and +50bp to 14.5% annual), and restaurant margins (+70bp to 14% quarter, and +50bp to 17.4% annual). For the year, KFC opened 1,340 net new stores, bringing the total store count to almost 13,000 units. Enhancing the profitability of KFC stores is the acceleration of the rollout of side-by-side KCOFFEE, KFC stores. Over 700 locations were added in 2025, bringing the total of these very profitable sites to 2200.

TABLE 8

Pizza Hut

Similarly, Pizza Hut's numbers showed consistent improvement. Same store sales were up +1% for both Q4 and the year, after negative numbers for 2024. Total system sales were also a bright spot, up 6% for the quarter and 4% for the year. Menu changes at Pizza Hut helped contribute to the system sales growth for the quarter. Operating margins continued to be tight for Pizza Hut, especially compared to KFC's. Restaurant margins were up 60 bp to 9.9% (quarter) and 80 bp to 12.8% (annual). Total operating margins increased 110 bp in the quarter to 3.7% and 110 bp (annual) to 7.9%.

Pizza Hut opened a record 444 net new stores in the years, bringing the total units to nearly 4,200. The operating margin of 7.9% for the year was a record for the Pizza Hut franchise since the spin-off from Yum Brands in 2016.

TABLE 9

Emerging Brands

Not much was mentioned on the conference call about the progress of new, "emerging" brands for the company, which has been somewhat of a disappointing subject. The only highlight was Lavazza, a 65/35% JV with Luigi Lavazza S.P.A., the Italian manufacturer of coffee products. The JV is intended to introduce the premium Lavazza coffee products to China through the development of free standing coffee cafes. Thirty-four new stores were opened in 2025, bringing the total to 146.

I was hoping for more progress in this "new concept" sector, as innovation is always welcomed, even in the fast food industry. Customer preferences change, and new food varieties can meet the challenges of those changes. Key to YUMC's growth to this point has been their adoption of new specialty items within the traditional KFC and Pizza Hut formats. A third profitable line could be within the new concept sector, but not much is happening yet.

Cash Return on Invested Capital (CROIC)

We are all aware of the importance of FCF to a firm's health, as it represents operating cash remaining after maintenance and growth capital expenditures have been deducted. It is cash available for use at management's discretion: to make acquisitions, to pay or increase cash dividends to stakeholders, or to repurchase shares in the open market. In my opinion, one of the most important metrics in analysis is cash return on invested capital (CROIC), as it compares FCF against the dollar value of capital employed in the firm from both debt and equity contributors. It not only measures the health of a firm but also its sustainability.

Shift to a Franchise Model - Enhancing CROIC

Table 10 presents YUMC's calculated CROIC for the 12-month periods ending 12/2016 through 12/2025 in comparison to that of four of its closest peers: McDonald's, Wendy's, Driden, and Restaurant Brands International (QSR). For those unfamiliar with QSR, it is a Canadian-based franchisor of the Burger King, Tim Hortons, and Popeyes fast food concepts. Note that the highest CROIC came from industry leader McDonald's, which utilizes the franchise form of business almost exclusively. Within the fast food industry, there are pros and cons to the development of a healthy business relying on company-owned stores compared to utilizing franchisee-operated units. Franchising is best defined as:

"a method of distribution in which the franchisor (the principal) passes along to the franchisee (the agent) business-specific information in return for a consideration usually in the form of fees and royalties."

TABLE 10

Franchising models do not always lead to enhanced returns. Corporate ownership provides increased revenues and direct oversight of store sites, though it also incurs substantial capital and maintenance expenses, resulting in reduced operating margins. Franchising yields lower revenue streams but steadier ones. It involves lower capital expenditures, and improved margins; however, the franchisor may experience diminished control over the store site and its product presentation, depending on the quality of the franchisee.

In my opinion, this discussion of company ownership versus franchising is important, as YUMC is moving towards a more frequent use of the franchise model with new store openings. Note in Table 10 MCD with 100% franchised units has the highest CROIC at 13.8% for the latest 12 months ending 12/2025. YUMC trails this performance at 10%, but has gradually improved its cash returns since the Covid debacle. I feel that with a move towards more franchised units, YUMC can begin to approach the 11-11.5% return marker over the next 3-5 year period.

The mix of franchise vs. company-owned new openings shifted in 2025 for YUMC, as the former model seemed to fit better with prospective franchisees in the smaller cities. The franchise mix for both KFC and Pizza Hut grew to 36% of new openings in 2025 vs. 25% in 2024. Still, the company-owned units represented about 80% of the total store count at year-end 2025.

Risks

Clearly, there are risks to my thesis, some of which are the same as in my earlier reports. First of all, the Chinese consumer remains challenged, with consumer confidence within the world's second-largest economy still low. As seen in Table 11, retail sales (both direct and online) have fallen sharply since the post-Covid lockdown.

TABLE 11

Fortunately for YUMC, its products remain at the lower end of the cost spectrum for discretionary spending by consumers. Its moderate acceleration in system sales growth gives me confidence that the Chinese populace has not lost its taste for KFC's "Soy Sauce Marinated Chicken", "Dragon Twister Wrap" or the localized toppings at Pizza Hut, such as crayfish, durian (a large tropical fruit), or even Peking Duck.

Regarding consumer demand, a longer-term risk, in my opinion, remains a saturation of the YUMC concepts within China. Again, YUMC's ability to open a large volume of stores on an annual basis, and maintain growth in operating and net profits makes this also a longer term risk. A mitigating factor would be accelerating growth in new concepts, something that I am disappointed to say has not yet occurred.

Finally, there is the risk that US/China relations deteriorate to the point where Chinese stocks are banned from listing on American exchanges. The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) established in 2020, and the more recently established Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act (HFCAA), were enacted to enhance the accuracy of foreign company financial disclosures by extending the ability of the PCAOB to examine the disclosure of firms listed on US exchanges. If the PCAOB determines that it is inhibited by any foreign jurisdiction in its examination privileges, the SEC must act to suspend trading in all firms domiciled in those jurisdictions.

At the time of the HFCAA's enactment in 2022, there were just under 200 Chinese-domiciled securities trading in the US. Today this number is approaching 300, with a market capitalization of just over $1.0 trillion. In mid-2022, the PCAOB reached an agreement with agencies of the Chinese government to allow regular full access to audit records, corporate management, and other pertinent topics to ensure alignment in China with US accounting and auditing standards.

Valuation - Price Target

As Table 11 indicates, there has been material growth in the street FYE2026 and 2027 estimates for YUMC over the past year: up almost 16% for 2026 and nearly 15% for 2027. The consensus estimates of $2.91 for 2026 and $3.34 for 2027 imply an expected 14.8% EPS growth rate, and at its current price of $53.65, YUMC trades at only a 16.1x forward multiple based on the 2027 estimate of $3.34.

TABLE 12

Given its expected growth rate and current price, YUMC trades at only a 1.09 PEG ratio, quite cheap, in my opinion, given its wide moat status, quality management, and dominant position in mainland China. I frankly think the 2026 and 2027 estimates are slightly low, and I am using $3.00 for 2026 and $3.45 for 2027. Putting an 18.5x multiple on my $3.45 estimate for 2026, I arrive at a one-year price target of $64, a price appreciation of about 18%.

Conclusion - Reiteration of Thesis

YUMC remains one of my largest personal holdings, and I reiterate the basics of my thesis: a dominant, wide moat fast food franchise; highly efficient operations with improving operating margins that should be enhanced by an accelerated move to higher margined franchised units; still tremendous growth in mainland China as it continues to penetrate 2nd and 3rd tier, smaller cities where it has little to no exposure; and a young vibrant management team that currently seems to be hitting on all cylinders.

One important item to mention in regard to the rationale for buying YUMC shares at this particular time is this: AI cannot create "original spicy old-fashioned Szechuan chicken" or "crackling golden chicken wings", two very popular products introduced by YUMC this year. YUMC is a quintessential HALO stock (using a term coined by Josh Brown of Ritholz Management). As previously mentioned, it means heavy asset, low obsolescence, and it fits.

I reiterate a strong buy on YUMC with a one-year price target of $64.

data.title: Inflation Is Real, And It's Spectacular
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Elections are a normal aspect of any democratic society, and elections around the world inevitably come with promises and carefully crafted narratives from every candidate on the ballot. It is also common to pick and choose metrics to highlight candidates in the most favorable light possible. This isn’t a Republican or Democrat phenomenon – it occurs across parties, ideologies, and levels of government throughout the democratic world.

My investment strategy doesn’t involve making moves based on political promises, slogans, or headlines; I invest based on facts, data, and cash flows. That approach guided my recent article on discounted income opportunities from New York City real estate, where we looked past political noise and fears, and focused instead on operational resilience. History has been very clear that markets ultimately respond to numbers, not narratives.

Today’s dominant political portrayal is that inflation is at its lowest level since the pandemic. While that may be true in a headline sense, the CPI report tells a more nuanced story once you look beneath the surface. Inflation has not disappeared; it has become selective. Certain categories continue to experience persistent price increases, particularly those tied to essential services and deeply ingrained consumer behavior. While broad averages mask these important realities, as investors, you can’t afford to ignore them.

Two of the clearest examples are tobacco and utilities. Tobacco prices continue to rise rapidly due to taxation, regulation, and inelastic demand. The January 2026 CPI report shows that tobacco and smoking product prices are up 8.5% YoY. Similarly, utilities face mounting costs from AI infrastructure investment, grid maintenance, and growing electricity demand, with 6.3% YoY increase in electricity costs and 10.8% YoY increase in piped natural gas costs, per the January 2026 CPI report.

These are not discretionary expenses and cannot be deferred. In this article, we’ll look at excellent income picks and examine why they remain well-positioned in an environment where inflation is still very real, even if it no longer makes the headlines.

Pick #1: UTG – Yield 5.9%

Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) manages $3.2 billion in total assets, and focuses almost entirely on utilities (62%), energy (10%), communication services (7%), and materials (7%). UTG maintains a concentrated portfolio of 63 holdings, with its top ten being among the largest utilities and biggest beneficiaries of data center contracts, representing 56% of the fund. Source

Over the past ten years, interest rates have fluctuated between extremes more than once. Yet, UTG has delivered excellent NAV growth while paying steady monthly distributions.

In fact, UTG has been delivering consistent monthly distributions since its inception in 2004, with its five-year distribution breakdown showing a large composition of long-term capital gains and qualified dividends.

UTG’s portfolio composition is mostly with companies based in the U.S., representing ~90% of the fund. The CEF operates with ~20% floating-rate leverage, with attractive borrowing costs at one month SOFR + 0.65%. UTG ended FY 2025 (year ended Oct 31, 2025) with a weighted average cost of its borrowings at a highly competitive 4.63%, and $558 million in unrealized gains, capable of covering 2.7 years of distributions. UTG currently trades at par with NAV.

We were not surprised when UTG delivered a distribution raise in mid-2025, and looking ahead, the CEF has a lot more potential backed by some of the most regulated cash cows in America.

Pick #2: MO – Yield 6.3%

When Altria Group, Inc. (MO) stock tanked after Q3 2025 earnings, we saw an opportunity to add to one of our favorite dividend grower. Our assessment was that the company’s raised earnings guidance and capital return plans present excellent value, despite Q3 numbers falling short of Wall Street estimates. The company’s Q4 and full-year earnings proved our conviction.

MO delivered Q4 Adj. EPS of $1.30/share, bringing the full-year total to $5.42/share, above the midpoint of the guidance range. Despite ongoing cigarette volume declines, MO continues to demonstrate strong pricing power, with price increases and cost discipline supporting margin preservation and steady EPS growth.

Through 2025, the company returned $8 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases, with $288 million in repurchases during Q4 ($1 billion in 2025). Altria ended 2025 with $1 billion remaining under its $2 billion share repurchase program that is available through 2026. $7 billion was returned to shareholders in 2025 in the form of dividend payments. Altria is a Dividend King, with 56 consecutive years of dividend raises.

Altria expects FY 2026 Adj. EPS between $5.56-$5.72/share, implying a 4% YoY growth at the midpoint. Notably, management expects a mid-single digit Adj. EPS CAGR and mid-single digit dividend growth through 2028. The company’s $1.06/share quarterly dividend annualizes to a 6.3% yield and can be placed at a respectable 75% payout ratio at the midpoint of the Adj. EPS guidance.

While MO has world-class cigarette brands like Marlboro, Copenhagen, and Black & Mild, the company has struggled to develop a market-leading smokeless product. Following the company’s ill-fated $12.8 billion investment in Juul in 2018, which was written off in 2023, MO made a 2.75 billion acquisition of NJOY Holdings in June 2023, as part of its “moving beyond smoking” initiative. Due to patent infringements, the ITC ordered the prohibition on the importation and sale of NJOY ACE products in the U.S. However, MO’s combustible business has been a resilient cash generator, supporting the company’s ambitions to compete in the smoke-free future. Altria and Japan Tobacco have a joint venture (with Altria being a 75% partner), Horizon Innovations, which has submitted pre-marketing applications for Ploom and Marlboro heated tobacco sticks.

In December, Altria’s Smoke-free subsidiary, Helix Innovations, reported that it received FDA marketing authorization for three flavors of its home-grown tobacco-leaf-free nicotine pouches, On! Plus. The company’s applications for six additional flavors are pending the FDA’s verdict, offering the potential for rebuilding market share in a rapidly growing category.

Altria ended 2025 with a leverage ratio of approximately 2.0x and $4.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Supported by an investment-grade balance sheet and highly recurring cash flows from its tobacco businesses, the company remains well-positioned to fund both shareholder returns and its expansion into emerging growth categories. Trading at just 11.3x forward earnings, Altria presents deep value and durable income in a market still priced for perfection.

Conclusion

George Costanza from Seinfeld has many passions – food, sex, and sports among them. These are all perfectly reasonable interests on their own. However, when he decided to combine them all into a single unified experience, it turned into a disaster. Not everything benefits from being mixed together.

Investing and politics share a similar problem. You may be deeply passionate about both, and they do overlap at times, but they operate by very different rules. Politics often revolves around narratives, timing, and persuasion, while investing is ultimately grounded in profitability, cash flows, and long-term value creation. Human emotions are common to both, and when investors try to merge them, the result is rarely good over the long term. Reacting to stories instead of studying fundamentals becomes an unhealthy distraction for someone with a long-term focus.

Inflation remains high across several non-negotiable bills. As investors, you can invest in companies that have excellent pricing power and are capable of passing on higher costs to consumers without sacrificing demand. In this article, we discuss two ways to profit from the surging cost of electricity and tobacco products. At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus is on securities that deliver abundant cash flows through dividends and interest payments, so our income stream is well-positioned to withstand inflationary pressures and enable a stress-free retirement. As a recipient of predictable cash flows, it is up to you to decide how much you want to spend or reinvest for compounded growth. This is the power of our Income Method; it puts you in the driver's seat of your financial future.

data.title: BlackRock: A Quality Compounder To Buy Now
data.description:
As a dividend growth investor, I'm not solely focused on a stock's starting yield. This is because, at 28 years old, I (Kody) presumably have plenty of time to compound.

In other words, I'm more focused on the trajectory of the underlying company and its payout over time. One key requirement for prospective investments is reliable earnings growth over 10 or 20 years (and decent forward-looking growth prospects). Combined with industry-safe payout ratios and investment-grade balance sheets, this can often fuel more dividend growth down the road.

That brings me to BlackRock (BLK), which will be the topic of today. When I last covered BlackRock with a Buy rating in December, I thought its outsized net inflows in Q3 2025 were a positive. The company's significant net cash and cash equivalents/investment balance was another. Relative to my fair value estimate, shares were also marginally undervalued at the time.

Two months later, I'm reaffirming my buy rating. BlackRock closed out 2025 with another quarter of impressive net inflows. This looks poised to continue as well. What's more, BlackRock could soon benefit from private equity/credit access being granted to 401(k) plans. That could provide a meaningful boost to its profitability. BlackRock's interest coverage ratio in 2025 was exceptionally strong. Shares are also a slightly better value now than they were in my prior article.

Continued Net Inflows And 401(k) Private Equity/Credit Are Major Growth Catalysts For BlackRock

On January 15th, BlackRock released its financial results for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company's total revenue surged 23.4% higher over the year-ago period to $7.01 billion in the quarter. Put into context, this surpassed Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $330 million.

What was behind BlackRock's topline growth for the fourth quarter? As has been the case in past quarters, growth in its assets under management was the driving force behind the company's greater revenue in the quarter. BlackRock's average AUM jumped 18.8% year-over-year to surpass $13.7 trillion during the quarter (while ending AUM vaulted 21.6% higher over the year-ago period to top $14 trillion).

Once again, the biggest contributor to BlackRock's swelling AUM had to do with market appreciation over the past 12 months. Strong fundamentals have pushed major market indices higher over that time.

What's more, the strength of BlackRock's product suite remained evident in Q4 2025. According to Chairman and CEO Larry Fink's opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, the company closed out the final quarter of 2025 by logging $342 billion in net inflows.

These sizable net inflows were broad-based, with its iShares ETF franchise contributing $181 billion during the fourth quarter. BlackRock's Cash Management products were a secondary contributor, posting $74 billion in net inflows in the quarter. Institutional active, private markets, and non-ETF retail products were tertiary contributors, making up the rest of net inflows for the quarter.

BlackRock's total net inflows through 2025 were a whopping $698 billion. For more perspective, the company's organic asset growth rate held steady at 6% throughout the year. Contributions from its GIP and HPS platforms contributed to yield expansion to close out 2025. This helped organic base fee growth (+9% YOY in Q4 2025) to outpace organic asset growth.

BlackRock's adjusted diluted EPS rose by 10.1% over the year-ago period to $13.16 during the fourth quarter. That topped the analyst consensus in the quarter by $0.95.

Moving forward, I'm confident that BlackRock has double-digit percentage annual adjusted diluted EPS growth ahead of itself over the next several years. What's my rationale?

Well, there's the fact that its core business is remarkably strong. The ongoing passive ETF trend helped iShares to post 12% organic asset and 13% organic base fee growth in 2025. As this trend continues, I foresee strength with iShares persisting into 2026 and beyond.

Then, there's the fact that BlackRock has now fully integrated GIP and HPS into its business. That should allow them to cut overlapping back-office costs in the near term.

Longer term, the growing mix from these businesses should also propel margins higher. BlackRock continues to target 45%+ adjusted operating margins (which would be an expansion over the 44.1% adjusted operating margin in 2025).

One last catalyst for the company is President Trump's executive order signed last August, opening up 401(k) accounts to private equity/private credit and infrastructure.

Shifting even a small fraction of its target date funds into private assets would unlock higher fees (private equity fees tend to be over 1% plus performance incentives, while ETF fees are usually under 10 basis points), while also still offering clients the higher returns to compensate for greater fees. In other words, the same dollar of AUM shifting into private alternatives could generate five to 10x more revenue for BlackRock than a low-cost ETF.

While a typical target-date fund would realistically allocate 5% to 10% to private alternatives due to ERISA liquidity requirements (and potentially up to 20% for younger participants), the fee differential means even a slight shift in allocation can disproportionately impact the bottom line.

For these reasons, I believe that the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for the asset manager is realistic.

In 2026, the current consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to rise by 12.6% to $54.14. For 2027, an additional 12.8% jump in adjusted diluted EPS to $61.07 is anticipated. In 2028, adjusted diluted EPS is expected to climb by another 15.1% to $70.28.

Finally, BlackRock is financially sound. The company's interest coverage ratio in 2025 was 117.4. This financial prowess earns it an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook. That gives it a low cost of capital to pursue periodic acquisitions to further strengthen its business (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to BlackRock's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and BlackRock's Q4 2025 Earnings Release Supplement).

The Industry Titan Is Currently Discounted

Since December, BlackRock's shares have returned 3%. That edged out the 2% total return posted by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Even after this modest rally, the asset manager looks like a decent value. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 19.5, which is the same as it was two months ago. For more perspective, this is below the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/E ratio of 20.7 (up slightly from 20.2 in December).

Looking ahead, I would argue that BlackRock can return to its 10-year average P/E ratio. That's because the 13.5% annual forward adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus of 13.5% points to the growth story holding up (the 10-year CAGR was 9.4%). Over the long term, this could even turn out to be more of a floor for BlackRock's fair value than its ceiling if 401(k) access to private equity/credit boosts margins as I expect.

As of today, the calendar year 2026 is 11% in the books. That suggests there's another 89% of 2026 and 11% of 2027 still ahead over the coming 12 months. This produces a forward 12-month adjusted diluted EPS input of $54.90.

Plugging this into our fair value P/E ratio of 20.7, I compute a fair value per share of $1,134. Compared to the current $1,069 share price (as of February 9th, 2026), that's equivalent to a 6% discount to fair value.

If BlackRock lives up to growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could deliver an 8% total return through 2026. By the end of 2031, BlackRock could generate 13% annual total returns.

Dividend Growth Is Back In A Big Way

BlackRock's 2.1% forward dividend yield registers about 60 basis points below the financial sector's 2.7% forward median yield. This represents a roughly 40 basis point narrowing of the spread from December. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System awards C- and D grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (up from D+ and D- grades in December).

Over the years, BlackRock has established itself as a reliable dividend grower. It has upped its payout to shareholders for 17 consecutive years, which outpaces the sector median of just 3 years. This is sufficient to earn an A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from December).

BlackRock is well-positioned to build on this dividend growth streak for the foreseeable future, too. The company's adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the mid-40% range for 2026. According to the Zen Research Terminal, that would be better than the 50% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer from the asset management industry.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal pegs the probability of a payout reduction within the next 12 months at only 1.1%. In the next average recession, this risk rises to a still modest 3.2%. These aspects of BlackRock's dividend lend credence to the Quant System's A+ grade for overall dividend safety (the same as it was in December).

Where I diverge from the Quant System is in terms of forward-looking dividend growth. The Quant System anticipates 6.7% annual forward dividend per share growth, which itself would be above the sector median of 4.3%.

I project that more annual dividend per share growth of around 10% is ahead. This is because the payout ratio is in a range where the dividend can realistically grow almost as fast as adjusted diluted EPS over the next few years. That's why I think the A- grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth is arguably too conservative (unchanged from December).

Risks To Consider

BlackRock is a company with tremendous tailwinds to power continued growth. However, the asset manager does face risks that could alter the investment thesis.

While President Trump's executive order opening up 401(k) accounts to private equity/credit undeniably creates lucrative opportunities for BlackRock, it still carries risk.

Since private assets don't have a daily market price, one risk is that the company's evergreen funds could face a period of underperformance or valuation disputes. That could trigger class-action lawsuits from 401(k) participants claiming a breach of fiduciary duty.

Expanding this risk, BlackRock operates in an industry in which client trust is of the utmost importance. Any major breach of fiduciary duty litigation or major cyber breaches could undermine client trust. Such instances could lead to client outflows and make it more difficult for BlackRock to grow in the future.

One final risk to the company is one that applies to all asset managers. The asset management industry depends on a steadily growing equity market. A prolonged market downturn could pressure BlackRock's financial results via reduced assets under management, net revenue, and adjusted diluted EPS.

Summary: BlackRock Is A High-Quality Dividend Compounder To Scoop Up Now

BlackRock's net inflows to close out 2025 point to an asset manager firing on all cylinders. The company's broad product portfolio should sustain this momentum. A market potentially opening up 401(k) plans to private equity/credit is the icing on the cake that could provide additional upside to profit margins. BlackRock's balance sheet remains a fortress as well.

Sealing the deal, shares are arguably a bit more undervalued now than they were a couple of months ago. This provides a path to 13% annual total returns over the medium term, which is why I'm standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: Virtus KAR Mid-Cap Core Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Technology stocks, particularly AI-related names, remained in the spotlight during the fourth quarter, with much market attention on the rally’s sustainability and the scale of capital expenditures. Investor concerns grew about whether outsized investments in AI would deliver expected returns, prompting some to reduce overweight tech positions, with interest broadening to other areas, including biotech and metals & mining stocks. Despite tech’s prominence, healthcare was the quarter’s top sector in the S&P 500® Index, which tracks 500 of the largest U.S. companies. Across market capitalizations, value stocks were favored over growth stocks, and investors appeared to be positioning for a cyclical upswing, supported by lower interest rates and expectations for accommodative fiscal policy.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Mid-Cap Core Fund (VIMCX) returned -2.44% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell Midcap® Index’s 0.16% return. Stock selection in information technology and financials detracted from performance, while stock selection in healthcare and an underweight position in real estate contributed positively to performance.

Cooper Companies and Ross Stores were the largest contributors to performance in the quarter.

  • Cooper Companies (COO), a global medical device company focused on vision care and women’s health, saw improved organic growth and improved guidance as its new MyDay silicone hydrogel disposable contact lenses rolled out and gained traction. Management also announced it was conducting some strategic review work to improve shareholder returns.
  • Discount retailer Ross Stores (ROST) reported improving results, driven by its branded strategy, store refresh initiatives, and new marketing campaigns. The company has provided guidance for comparable sales growth above peers and is benefiting from increased closeout availability and ongoing consumer trade-down.

Houlihan Lokey and Pool Corporation were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Investment bank Houlihan Lokey (HLI) grew revenues in both its corporate finance and financial restructuring businesses. During the quarter, market participants became concerned about the industry-wide impact the government shutdown could have on mergers and acquisitions activity and the potential resultant effects on the company’s deal-driven business.
  • Pool Corporation (POOL), a wholesale distributor of swimming pool supplies and equipment, reported flat sales due to headwinds from the deflation in pool chemical prices caused by a competitive environment.

data.title: The AI Market Crash Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Last Summer, we warned investors that AI was coming for their portfolio.

Back then, the market appeared to still think of AI as a catalyst for most stocks, as it had the potential to increase productivity, lower costs, and increase margins.

That may be true over the short-term.

However, the concerning thing is that AI also breaks traditional barriers to entry, which will likely lead to much greater competition over time, hurting long-term profitability.

The market is now finally starting to wake up to this risk, and software companies are the first to take it on the chin.

Many of the big and well-established software companies like Salesforce (CRM), Intuit (INTU), Workday (WDAY), Roper Technologies (ROP), and Constellation Software (CNSWF) have seen their share prices drop 30-50% since we pointed to this risk:

If the big names are down so much, you can imagine that the smaller software companies must be down even more. Monday (MNDY), Hims (HIMS), and Duolingo (DUOL), as an example, are down 70-80% from their peaks, despite earning record profits:

Software companies have been the most affected by this sell-off so far because digital businesses are the easiest to replicate with the help of AI.

What used to require a team of developers, a few million dollars, and many months to build can now be replicated in an afternoon by a vibe-coder using AI.

That's how powerful it is.

So yes, AI may help these companies reduce costs and increase profits over the short term, but there is no doubt that this will also lead to much greater competition over time, which will put pressure on pricing power and retention rates.

The market is finally recognizing this problem, and the impact on valuations has already been massive.

But Here's What the Market is Still Overlooking:

I think that software companies are just the tip of the iceberg.

I expect the AI revolution to ultimately hurt most companies and their stocks.

The first domino to fall was digital businesses because they are the easiest to replicate by new competitors with AI, but this is still just the beginning.

Over time, I expect most businesses to be impacted by this. Some more than others, of course, but very few businesses will be completely immune to this.

In most fields, new and more efficient AI-powered companies will emerge and start to compete with older and more bloated incumbents.

Let's look at some other examples of businesses that will likely get disrupted as AI breaks down traditional barriers to entry:

  • Professional service providers: Think of law firms. They used to charge a lot of money for answering fairly basic questions and drafting simple contracts. Now, AI does it for free all the time. I know this because I run a small business myself, and it saves me a lot of money. But it is not just that. AI also helps law firms resolve more complex issues much more efficiently, and this breaks barriers to entry in the process. As an example, reviewing a complex contract may have taken a day for a junior lawyer in the past. Now it may take just a few minutes to find what you are looking for. Therefore, the scale of a large law firm is not a major competitive advantage anymore. Expect far more lawyers to go independent and set up their own shops, resulting in far more competition in the future. More complex tasks will get cheaper, and simple tasks will become free. The same applies to most professional service providers, including marketing agencies, consultants, accountants, etc.
  • Education: I would bet that the traditional education system of having an entire class sitting in a room and listening to a teacher/professor is not as effective as having an AI-powered tutor that's perfectly personalized to your level of knowledge and pace of learning that also teaches you in a way that's more engaging to keep you focused. But despite being far less effective, the traditional education system is also far more expensive. Disruption is coming.
  • Healthcare: A lot of basic healthcare questions are already being answered by AI rather than doctors today, but this is just the beginning. Radiologists are also being replaced by AIs that are far more precise. In the future, robots will even perform surgeries a lot better than surgeons themselves.
  • Traditional retail: We all know that e-commerce disrupts traditional retail by pulling shoppers away from stores. This has been happening for a long time already. But with AI now making it easier than ever before for anyone to start an online business while also making online marketing much more personalized and effective, and therefore, I expect the growth of e-commerce to only accelerate from here, leading to even greater disruption to traditional retail businesses. E-commerce companies will themselves also suffer as they will face greater competition than ever before.
  • Consumer brands: With AI making it far easier to compare prices, products, reviews, etc. I expect the loyalty of consumers and the value of brands to go down, even as white-label alternatives will gain in popularity. It is today also easier than ever before to launch your own micro-brand with great marketing, logo design, customer service, etc., from day 1, which will inevitably lead to greater competition.
  • Entertainment: Producing movies and video games used to cost tons of money. Now, you can do it at a tiny fraction of the cost. The vast majority of online content is already AI-produced. The impact on movie studios, game developers, movie theaters, and most other entertainment businesses, etc., could be significant as there is ever more competition for your attention.
  • Transportation: Autonomous vehicles are already here, and in a not-so-distant future, most new vehicles will likely be autonomous. This also means that people will increasingly not need to own their personal cars. There will be an abundance of cars driving around, picking up and dropping off people at a very low cost. Autonomous vehicles have also been shown to have 5-10x fewer accidents on average. This could be massively disruptive to many industries: fewer car sales, lower demand for convenience stores, fewer accidents, lower demand for auto parts and repair shops, insurance companies face disruption, etc.
  • Platforms: Will we still need platforms like Uber (UBER) to order a ride in a world in which car manufacturers like Tesla (TSLA) build their own apps to order robotaxis? Will we still need Google (GOOG) to search for something when ChatGPT can answer us instantly? Will we still need DoorDash (DASH) if you can ask an AI to directly place an order with the restaurant for you? Maybe. But will their profitability decline? I would expect so.

The point here is that software companies are likely just the first domino to fall. I expect most businesses to suffer over time as AI breaks traditional moats and commoditizes businesses.

I expect it to lead to a boom in new small business formation, increasing competition, and reducing the profitability of most businesses over time.

And So, With That in Mind, How Do You Protect Yourself From This Potential Value Destruction?

You need to identify those businesses that will be better protected than others, and it is not easy.

Generally speaking, I think that the more a business is tied to the physical real world, the better protected it will be.

A good example of that would be McDonald's (MCD). They control some of the best real estate throughout the world, have built a powerful brand, and offer convenience at a reasonable cost.

That's hard to disrupt. More competition and lower brand loyalty could impact them over time, but this won't happen any time soon.

Another good example would be commodities businesses. Enterprise Product Partners (EPD), for example, owns and operates a vast network of energy pipelines that is essential to our society. The need for energy is not going anywhere, and EPD is well-protected against short-term commodity fluctuations. The main risk is that AI and robotics could, over time, bring down the cost of extracting these resources. Again, this is not a near-term risk but something to keep in mind.

But the ultimate anti-AI asset class, in my opinion, is REITs (VNQ). It is the polar opposite of anything "artificial" in that they own "real" properties that cannot be easily replicated.

They are limited in supply by their locations, building permits, capital availability, and construction costs. Yet, well-located properties grow in demand over the long run, increasing their profitability and value.

People will always need to have a roof over their head and places to store their goods.

The need for things like apartment communities, single-family homes, warehouses, distribution centers, self-storage facilities, cell towers, data centers, grocery-anchored strip centers, net lease properties, timberland, farmland, billboards, hotels, skilled nursing facilities, emergency rooms, etc., will never go away.

Despite that, REITs are today heavily discounted due to many misconceptions.

One of them is that REITs are heavily exposed to offices and malls, which face an uncertain future. In reality, those REITs represent less than 5% of the REIT sector, and even those REITs are doing fine for the most part, as they own primarily Class A properties in desirable locations.

Many people also think that REITs will suffer due to the surge in interest rates. In reality, REITs use little leverage, with an average 35% LTV, and this explains why cash flows and dividends have kept steadily rising even in recent years.

Despite that, as we have often discussed, REIT valuations are today historically low:

My prediction is that as these recent AI fears spread from software to other sectors over time, investors will become increasingly concerned about wealth preservation and increase allocations to anti-AI asset classes like REITs.

This will then change the narrative, their market sentiment will improve, and their valuations will rise back closer to historic norms, or even higher.

Today, investors still have the chance to buy shares of high-quality REITs like Kimco Realty (KIM), Healthcare Realty (HR), and BSR REIT (BSRTF / HOM.U:CA) at large discounts relative to the fair value of their assets, but potentially not for much longer. REITs are up 7% YTD on average, even as SaaS companies have crashed, and this capital rotation to AI-proof asset classes is still just getting started:

We are accumulating as much as we can of these discounted REITs at High Yield Landlord before they recover.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Zero-Capital Leach Growth, Hidden Shadow Mine Advantage
data.description:
A buy—that is what I assign to Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) stock. My investment thesis here focuses on a large valuation arbitrage. I am not pricing Freeport-McMoRan stock as a traditional capital-intensive miner, considering the shadow mine inherent in its Americas Leach Innovation Initiative. This initiative unlocks 42 billion pounds of copper from existing stockpiles with negligible CapEx. This will be altering Freeport-McMoRan's ROIC profile. Beyond the headline risk of the Grasberg mudflow recovery, I do not underestimate the structural margin expansion derived from converting waste rock into 800 million pounds of annual production by 2030. This is a volume that is exceeding major greenfield projects without the decade-long permitting and billion-dollar CapEx drag. However, there are risks to my thesis that include the geotechnical fragility of the Grasberg Softzone (Production Block 1C). A failure to successfully isolate this unstable geological domain during the H2-FY2026 ramp-up can sterilize important gold-rich reserves and compress FCX stock's valuation multiples.

Valuation Advantage of the Shadow Mine

Freeport-McMoRan's stock price can go considerably higher over the long term amid the strong undervaluation of the Americas Leach Innovation Initiative. This initiative functions as a massive zero-capital-intensity shadow mine hidden within Freeport-McMoRan's existing waste rock. I am going beyond focusing on the cyclicality of copper prices and the geopolitical volatility of Indonesia, as the internal data indicates a structural arbitrage of capital intensity that builds a high upside. As per Q4-FY2025, Freeport sits on an identified resource of ~42 billion pounds of contained copper in stockpiles that was deemed unrecoverable. The innovation initiative (utilizing proprietary additives and heat injection) attained a run rate of 240 million pounds per annum in late 2025 and is targeting growth to 800 million pounds per annum by 2030.

For more clarity on the magnitude of this value, I compare the capital intensity of this shadow mine against traditional organic growth. The Bagdad 2X expansion needs an estimated $3.5 billion in CapEx to generate an incremental 200 to 250 million pounds of copper per year. Against that, the leach initiative targets 800 million pounds per annum (that is ~3.5x the volume of the Bagdad expansion) with negligible incremental capital compared to the billions needed for greenfield or brownfield mill concentrator expansions. If Freeport were to build traditional mine capacity to drive this 800 million pounds of copper, depending on the Bagdad 2X capital intensity ratio (3.5 billion for ~225 million lbs), it might theoretically need over $12 billion in CapEx and nearly a decade of permitting and construction. Hence, the leach initiative delivers this volume capacity utilizing sunk costs (already mined material) and OpEx, which breaks the traditional miner's dilemma of dropping ROCE.

More so, the 2026 guidance indicates a target of 300 million pounds from leaching, which is a 40% Y/Y increase. This validates the tech efficacy of the internally developed additives and heat retention strategies currently being deployed at Morenci and El Abra. As this production forms a new baseline, it reduces the consolidated unit net cash costs (projected at $1.75 per pound for 2026) by diluting fixed costs over a larger low-variable-cost volume base. Beyond pricing FCX stock based on the risk-adjusted cash flows of its hard-rock mining ops (Grasberg and US concentrators), in my opinion, FCX stock is yet to price-in the conversion of 42 billion pounds of waste into a Tier 1 asset that needs no digging, no milling, and no tailings expansion. As the run rate approaches 800 million pounds by 2030, the expansion of FCF margins based on this zero-CapEx volume will force a permanent upward re-rating of FCX stock's multiple. That can be treating this segment closer to a tech-enabled royalty than a capital-intensive extraction operation (in my opinion).

For the CapEx profile, Freeport-McMoRan indicates 2026 CapEx of ~$4.3 billion, which holds $1.3 billion for stabilizing other projects and $3 billion for major growth (as per Q4-IR-deck). Mainly, the Leach Innovation Initiative needs <$1 billion in total incremental investment. This CapEx fraction can yield superior capital efficiency against the $3.5 billion required for traditional mill expansions like Bagdad. Whereas for Copper demand, supply, and price outlook, as per S&P Global, copper demand may surge 50% to 42 million metric tons by 2040, building a long-term 10-million-ton deficit led by AI and electrification. This imbalance supports higher long-term pricing. Along with that, Freeport-McMoRan's sensitivity models mark annual operating cash flows of ~$11 billion at recent spot prices of $5.75/pound. As per Goldman Sachs, copper prices may hit $12,115/tonne by FY2029 and expand more in 2030.

Geotechnical Fragility of Grasberg

To push the FCX stock price downside, there is a risk. It is the unpriced geotechnical fragility of the Grasberg Block Cave [GBC] Production Block 1C [PB1C] and the high-probability risk that the 2025 mudflow incident indicates a permanent instead of a temporary impairment of the mine's nameplate capacity. From my perspective, the consensus treats the September 2025 mud rush as a discrete operational event to be remediated by plugs and pumping. However, there is a structural issue. The PB1C area is in a geological domain termed the 'Softzone' due to low-strength clay-rich material. The incident is not an operational error, in my opinion. In my stance, there is a formation of a pathway along the cave boundary that is connecting the extraction level to 800K metric tons of wet mud accumulation at the surface. This indicates that the cave propagation mechanics in this sector may possibly be unstable and different from the competent rock in other blocks.

Alongside that, Freeport-McMoRan's recovery plan creates an extreme execution cliff in H2-FY2026. The 2026 sales guidance is heavily back-weighted, with 60% of copper sales and 75% of gold sales projected for H2. This projection depends mostly on the successful Q2-FY2026 restart of Production Blocks 2 and 3 and the installation of cement plugs to isolate PB1C. However, the proximity of PB1C to the active blocks, combined with the interconnectivity of the cave subsidence zone, holds a risk that drawing ore from adjacent blocks (PB 2/3 or PB 1S) can destabilize the compromised Softzone. It may trigger more liquefied material ingress. A restart of PB1C is only a 'potential' by year-end 2027, and management is 'evaluating' if they will ever return to PB1C. If PB1C is abandoned, ~10% of the GBC's production footprint is sterilized.

More negatively, if the isolation of PB1C fails to prevent fluid communication with the surface during the ramp-up of Blocks 2 and 3. Apart from that, the resulting downtime can occur when Freeport-McMoRan has guided for peak gold sales (1.2 million ounces annualized average 2026-2030). The operational leverage at Grasberg is immense, fixed costs are high, and the mine accounts for the majority of Freeport-McMoRan's gold revenue (a $4,000/oz case adds $120 million EBITDA per $100/oz change). A failure to meet the H2-FY2026 ramp-up due to recurring geotechnical instability in the Softzone can lead to missing the 2026 guidance of 3.4 billion pounds and may force a structural downgrade of the GBC's long-term recoverable reserves and maximum daily throughput. This factor can crack Wall Street's confidence in the world's largest gold mine story. This can compress FCX stock valuation largely as the market reprices Grasberg from a stable cash cow to a geologically compromised asset requiring perpetual capital-intensive remediation.

~65% Upside

To value Freeport-McMoRan stock, I am utilizing a normalized stable EV/EBITDA framework from the provided 2027-2028 operational outlook, adjusted for the superior quality of leach-derived cash flows. At a copper price of $5 per pound, which is conservative relative to spot prices of $5.75, Freeport-McMoRan projects an average annual EBITDA of ~$15 billion for the FY2027-FY2028. I am applying an 8x average forward EV/EBITDA multiple that is justified by the increasing proportion of zero-capital leach volume that should have a technology-like premium. This yields an Enterprise Value of ~$120 billion.

Now, deducting the net debt of $2.3 billion (excluding the ring-fenced downstream project debt) drives an equity value of ~$117.7 billion. With 1.44 billion diluted shares outstanding, this intrinsic valuation indicates a mid-term fair value target of ~$81.7 per share. In a bullish commodity cycle scenario where copper stabilizes at $6 per pound, the projected annual EBITDA expands to over $19 billion. Holding the multiple constant, this drives an equity value of ~$104 per share (+65% price upside). Current market pricing does not isolate and premium-weight the leach-wedge of production and treat high-margin recovery pounds the same as capital-heavy mill pounds.

To verify technically (based on the weekly chart), Freeport-McMoRan stock is showing parabolic climax structure within the current primary uptrend. FCX stock has breached the Pivot ($61.81), closing at $62.84, which is confirming strong accumulation. The bullish momentum is pointed out by the MACD that is expanding vertically at 5.408 since the breakout above the 2025 consolidation zones. However, the technicals are overextended. The stock is trading nearly 15% above its 13-week EMA ($54.58), which creates a large mean-reversion air pocket. Along with that, the RSI (13) has hit 79.06, which is indicative of overbought state conditions that historically precede corrections/consolidations to digest gains.

Lastly, most alarmingly, there is a subtle bearish divergence forming on the Full Stochastic that has rolled over (73.4) and crossed below its signal line amid FCX price making new highs. This disconnect between price action and momentum marks buyer exhaustion. Although the trend is strongly bullish, the setup means a possible tactical pullback to test support at the 5-week EMA ($59.99) or 13-week EMA ($54.58). The current zone indicates a risk entry as it is a technical area for booking partial profits.

Earnings Alpha Protocol

Buying FCX stock is logical Pre-Q1-FY2026 to capitalize on the anticipated negative sentiment near the guided trough in operational metrics. Freeport-McMoRan's management has guided for elevated unit net cash costs of $2.6 per pound and suppressed sales volumes (640 million pounds of copper) for Q1 due to the Grasberg downtime. This creates a predictable, apparently poor quarter that will likely depress FCX stock price. For bulls, it may be an important contrarian entry point. Post-Q1 2026, the long-side strategy must move to verification. To hold the long-position, there has to be a restart in the Production Blocks 2 and 3 in Q2-FY2026. If the Softzone plugs hold and the leach initiative tracks toward its 300 million pound 2026 target, Wall Street may price-in the H2-FY2026 volume inflection (60% of sales guided for H2), and this can trigger the re-rating.

_

With all that, Freeport-McMoRan stock is one to own as a Tier 1 copper asset that is shifting its capital efficiency through the technological monetization of waste rock. The Buy rating is based on Freeport-McMoRan's capability to add equivalent production of a major new mine without the related capital destruction. For the long-term, I am laser-focused on two specific monitorables. One is the geotechnical integrity of the isolation plugs in the Grasberg Softzone during the Q2 restart. The second one is the specific quarterly run-rate growth of the leach initiative against the 800 million pound long-term target. Progress in these 2 areas ticks the structural shift in FCF generation.
